text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, Chapter Background — p.134.

Chapter 3 — Escape from Old Korvosa. The queen's madness blossoms as Kazavon's fragment quickens within her. Quarantined Old Korvosa rots under mob rule — and somewhere in it hides the city's salvation: the missing seneschal, Neolandus Kalepopolis, the one man who can legally depose a corrupt monarch. PCs begin at 8th level → 9th after the Emperor of Old Korvosa → 10th before the Vivified Labyrinth → nearly 11th by chapter's end.

Kazavon's whispers (GM only). For centuries the fangs whispered to sensitive souls, breeding madmen instead of servants. When Ileosa freed the dragon's spirit-fragment, its seven current 'projects' lost their muse — six killed themselves; the seventh is the painter Salvator Scream, Pilts Swastel's gore-artist at Exemplary Execrables.

The Neolandus chain (the chapter's spine): the wounded seneschal fled the Red Mantis to his secret friend Salvator → Salvator hid him with the Arkonas (patron 'Glorio Arkona' — actually the rakshasa Bahor) → the Arkonas now cage Neolandus as an insurance policy against the queen. Vencarlo, investigating, was ambushed by the Red Mantis (they suspect he is Blackjack), survived, tried to infiltrate Arkona Palace — and is now a prisoner alongside the man he sought. The trail the PCs must follow: Vencarlo's home → Salvator → Pilts → the Arkonas.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, The Crown Revealed — p.136.

As the plague breaks, Queen Ileosa — wearing a new crown set with curved fangs — calls a surprise public address. Flanked by Sabina Merrin, the bloatmage seneschal Togomor, Cressida Kroft, and Sable Company commandant Marcus Endrin, she declares victory over blood veil (deftly spinning Davaulus's fate), names the Gray Maidens Korvosa's official protectors with Sabina as general — and disbands the Sable Company, seizing its holdings and demanding Endrin surrender his badge.

The assassination attempt. Endrin steps forward, trembling — then hurls his badge into the queen's face, bellows that her shameful reign ends today, and puts an arrow into her right temple before anyone can move. A perfect, fatal shot, sunk deep. Ileosa does not fall. Relay what the stunned witnesses saw (the PCs should hear all of this secondhand — time it just after Davaulus dies, while they are still mopping up the temple below the hospice):

With incredible speed, she regains her composure and yanks the arrow from her skull. Before blood from the wound has time to run all the way down to her jaw, she's standing before Endrin. Her free hand whips out and seizes him by the throat, lifting him off the ground and holding him up for all to see. An instant later, she stabs Endrin's own arrow up under his jaw and out the side of his face with a spray of blood.

As Endrin's body crumples to the ground and Ileosa imperiously shakes his blood from her hand, she cries out in a strong, clear voice, 'This shall be the fate of all enemies of Korvosa! Mark well his punishment! It is only the first!' A moment later, Togomor steps forward, taking Ileosa's hand and teleporting her back into the castle. The resulting riot is quelled quickly and brutally by the Gray Maidens. Field Marshal Cressida Kroft flees to Citadel Volshyenek, stunned and horrified by what she witnessed, and knowing that things have indeed taken a turn for the worse in her city. (Endrin is not killed by this turn of events, and the PCs may be able to rescue him from the Longacre Building at a later date-see Chapter 4).

This is the campaign's mid-point reveal: Ileosa is now visibly superhuman, the Gray Maidens are the law, and open opposition is suicide. The city reels — their queen cannot be killed. The Crown of Fangs artifact debuts here; the queen retreats from public view until Chapter 6's endgame begins to turn.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, The Third Harrowing — p.136.

Best timed soon after the Urgathoan cult's defeat. Zellara senses Ileosa's gathering strength — though not yet what it signifies — and uses her empathic link to plant an itch in the deck-bearer's mind: deal the cards. If the PC doesn't oblige within a day or two, she takes matters into her own hands the next time the PCs are alone, manifesting via major image and performing the reading herself.

The Third Harrowing. This reading generates the chapter's Harrow Points (Conversion Guide → The Harrowing). This chapter is tied to the suit of books and to Intelligence — a chapter of masks, lies, and puzzles rewarded for thinking.

Interpreting the spread:

  • Past: dwell on despots — cruel overlords and dictators, drawn from the campaign so far or from each PC's own history.
  • Present: Korvosa's wounds — oppression, martial law, and the dawning certainty that the queen herself may want the PCs dead.
  • Future: be grim — ghosts; demonic beasts (tigers and mantises especially); life as a cruel, vindictive play or other work of art; and the recurring image of beautiful women with ashen skin and blank faces (the Gray Maidens).

The Rakshasa card. If it appears anywhere in the spread, give it special weight: depending on its position, play up its meanings of dominance or of freedom from enslavement. The players won't understand until Arkona Palace — then they'll remember.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, Whispers from Old Korvosa — p.138.

Soon after, a haggard Cressida summons the PCs to a nearly deserted Citadel Volshyenek and, behind a mage's private sanctum, lays it out: Korvosa is being murdered by its queen, and only the PCs can act.

'Korvosa is dying. No, strike that. Korvosa is being murdered. Killed by our queen. The evidence you've uncovered that links her to the plague is damning enough, but now that she's disbanded the Sable Company and reallocated our own funding to the Gray Maidens, she's more in control now than ever. I dare not move against her, as my guards would be executed to the last fighter by her Gray Maidens before sundown. But she must be stopped. And I know of no one else but you to do this deed.

'You see, just this morning, new information came to me. Vencarlo Orisini's been one of my most trusted sources of information regarding the public; it's hard for an officer of the Korvosan Guard to get honest opinions from the citizens, and Vencarlo's observations on these topics have been a godsend, particularly since the plague started. When the queen quarantined Old Korvosa, I'd feared his messages would end, yet he managed to find ways to smuggle updates to me every day.

Recently, he wrote of discovering something of vital importance regarding the queen. He mentions something about dark magic and a pact with a devil, but until the events of this morning, I found his claims difficult to believe. But even more astounding, he hinted he'd found a lead on Seneschal Kalepopolis, and implied the man might still be in hiding in Old Korvosa!

The mission: infiltrate quarantined Old Korvosa, find Vencarlo (start at his home by the Orisini Academy), and trace Neolandus — whose return would give the queen's opponents a legal lever against her. Cressida warns of the new 'Emperor of Old Korvosa' holding the streets. Ironically the island is the safest place for queen-hunted PCs — if they keep a low profile and avoid Gray Maidens. Timeline note: Vencarlo's daily smuggled messages ran unbroken from the quarantine's start until several days ago — dating his disappearance precisely.

Cressida's cache (the citadel's last reserves): 4 potions of cure serious wounds (moderate healing potions), 3 potions of lesser restoration, a wand of invisibility (10 charges), and a wand of cure moderate wounds (30 charges).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, Old Korvosa Today — p.139.

Endrin Isle is sealed: the Gray Maidens burned every wooden bridge, walled the stone Jeggare Bridge with a 15-ft wall of stone barricade, and patrol the Narrows in swift barges. Anyone may enter; no one may leave — escapees are warned once, then fired upon.

The four wards: Fort Korvosa (highest, most orderly — the Arkonas' redoubt) · Garrison Hill (burned, scavenger-haunted) · Bridgefront (worst-off, morale shattered) · Old Dock (the most dangerous — the Emperor's domain).

Getting in/out: flight or teleportation trivializes it; otherwise a night swim or skiff across the northern Jeggare (@Check[type:athletics|dc:15] to swim quietly; @Check[type:stealth|dc:18] to evade the barge patrols), returning via the dark, half-burned far side of the Narrows. Don't over-police it — any half-decent plan works.

Strange times in Old Korvosa — color the streets with the desperate and the broken whenever the PCs travel:

Children : A flock of incongruously happy children sing a strange little rhyme as they gather around something in the middle of the street and sing, 'Headless, headless, that's what you'll be, brand-new dolls in the Emp'ror's cemet'ry! Choppy, choppy, chop, the tall knife calls, waitin' for the day for Korvosa to fall.' One of the children has fashioned a crude guillotine from sticks and pegs, and the children are using it to behead a collection of dolls. If the PCs speak to the children, they brag that they've been to see the emperor's real 'tall knife' at his palace, when in fact this is just false bravado.

  • Rook, the mad plague doctor (@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.queensPhysician1]{Queen's Physician (2)}): the highest Perception roll in the party hears a chirping, cooing voice — more human than bird — from a nearby alley. Within crouches a man in a filthy beaked mask. The plague broke his mind: he remembers nothing of his old life and believes himself a birdlike psychopomp called Rook. He offers to 'breathe in the sick' from the PCs so they never fall ill again; the 'cure' does nothing, and at the GM's discretion close contact may expose them to something worse.
  • Old Mother Mifeg (a sad old fungus-seller): shuffles the street edges hawking dully colored pigment-fungi, sobbing that she may not earn enough coppers to feed her six children. The truth is crueler — she is her family's only blood veil survivor, and the rotting bodies she tends so lovingly at home are, to her, merely 'sleeping off the sick.'
  • Tesh Zobberdin (a doomed avenger): wild-eyed in ill-fitting leather, marching on Old Dock with sword drawn — last of a family the Emperor executed. He is hopelessly outmatched, but could serve the PCs as a guide.
  • Olmere Bliversin (the ranting baker): harangues the empty street from his balcony — what will the queen do about the mob? the bridges? the quarantine? — in his nightclothes, every demand punctuated by a high-pitched giggle.

The mob. Unless the PCs stay unobtrusive, each hour carries a 10% chance (50% per 10 minutes in Old Dock) that mob soldiers notice them — four @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thugs}, arrogant and jeering, demand to know their business in 'the emperor's domain.' Any answer except 'We're here to join you' draws scorn and an order to hand over weapons. PCs who comply are escorted to the throne balcony (C3) to be judged by the Emperor, their gear stacked beside his throne as an offering. The mob should be the PCs' first encounter; afterwards roll on the Korvosa Street Encounter table (Appendices).

  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thug (5)}
Pilts's Mob (recurring, in waves)Low · 60 XP @ L8

4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thugs} — the standard shakedown. They open with nonlethal fists (live captives amuse the Emperor) and draw axes only if the PCs fight lethally; each flees at ~10 HP. Escalation: if the PCs cut down the first group, two more groups of four converge 3d6 minutes later (Severe · 120 XP @ L8); if those fall too, a fourth group of four arrives in another 3d6 minutes — this one extending a wary invitation to meet the Emperor. PCs who accept keep their gear, but are shadowed by thugs all the way to the palace.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, A — Vencarlo's Home — p.141.

A humble house in southern Fort Korvosa, beside the charred ruin of the Orisini Academy (burned by the Red Mantis after Vencarlo escaped their ambush). The door is unlocked; the rooms are warm, quiet, unlit — and both fireplaces are burning in an 'empty' house.

Reading the scene: @Check[type:perception|dc:24] (in A7/A8; +10 with scent) catches a strange-tanged smoke; @Check[type:crafting|dc:20] identifies residual alchemist's fire, and @Check[type:perception|dc:26] finds it faintly staining walls, floor, and furniture. @Check[type:survival|dc:24]: no real traffic since Vencarlo's last letter; @Check[type:survival|dc:29]: faint marks of a very stealthy recent visitor.

The rooms (A1–A8): a Vudrani-rug entry · a blade-repair workshop · bath · a study of fencing and philosophy texts (the academy's books show business dying after the king's death) · pantry · the living room (lit hearth, hiding-nook under the stairs) · the double-height training room (lit hearth, open rafters) · Vencarlo's unslept-in bedroom.

The ambush. Two @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.redMantisAssn001]{Red Mantis assassins} squat here in 8-hour shifts (relieved from the guild's hideout below the Longacre Building — a Chapter 4 thread), waiting for Vencarlo. One hides under the A6 stairs, one in the A7 rafters. They pre-buff the moment anyone enters and spring when a PC climbs to A7 — opening by igniting both fireplaces into the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.spreadingFire001]{Spreading House Fire}.

  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.redMantisAssn001]{Red Mantis Assassin (9)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.spreadingFire001]{Spreading House Fire (8)}
Red Mantis ambushSevere · 120 XP @ L8

2 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.redMantisAssn001]{Red Mantis assassins} + the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.spreadingFire001]{fire} they are warded against. A telegraphed Severe: the fire punishes slow play, and the assassins use it as terrain.

The Blackjack cache. @Check[type:perception|dc:29] in the A8 closet reveals a hidden panel; the iron lockbox within (@Check[type:thievery|dc:40], or Hardness 10 / 40 HP — the key is with Vencarlo's confiscated gear in the Arkona vaults, D16) holds a bag of holding (type I) containing Blackjack's full regalia: a black hooded cloak of elvenkind, several masks, 12 masterwork 'B'-pommeled daggers, +1 resilient slick leather armor, boots of elvenkind, an amulet of proof against detection, black gloves of climbing and swimming (two fingers of the right glove false and adjustable — concealing Vencarlo's old dueling wound), and a +2 striking keen rapier. @Check[type:society|dc:15] (automatic for anyone who saw the execution rescue) recognizes Blackjack's signature gear.

The Blackjack legacy. The mantle has passed hero-to-hero for generations; Vencarlo is merely its latest bearer. With this cache, a PC could become the next Blackjack — encourage it; the regalia is sized to fit its wearer. If the PCs don't fight the fire, the house burns (citizens + Arkona brigades contain the blaze but can't save it; the lockbox survives, @Check[type:perception|dc:26] in the rubble).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, The Concerned Student — p.145.

Drawn by the flames or the fight, a familiar face approaches: Amin Jalento (the noble the PCs may have saved from the Chapter 1 mob — or his cousin Gerran, if Amin died), trapped on the island by the quarantine and squatting in a collapsed building across from the academy.

His story: he was Vencarlo's houseguest when Red Mantis assassins stormed the academy — Vencarlo cut one down and fled; the assassins burned the school. Before that, Vencarlo kept strange hours, came home bloodied, and met three times behind closed doors with a wild-haired, paint-stained visitor Amin recognized: the notorious artist Salvator Scream. Their last meeting ended in shouting. Salvator lives at 140 Wave Street, Old Dock — the Emperor's territory.

Story awards: learning of Salvator Scream → 2,400 XP; escorting Amin safely off the island → a further 4,800 XP.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, B — The Artist's Lair — p.145.

B. The Artist's Lair — Salvator Scream's leaning, decrepit house at 140 Wave Street, Old Dock, near a ruined Narrows bridge. @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:15] (gather information) confirms the address and the artist's grisly reputation (Exemplary Execrables' backdrop painter); @Check[type:society|dc:22] knows who Salvator Scream is outright. A locate creature would reveal Salvator is no longer here — the Emperor's thugs took him days ago.

Who's actually home: @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori Vaus}, squatting amid her investigation. She is friendly unless attacked — let her be the PCs' strangest handshake of the campaign.

Laori's hidden layer (GM only). The chipper elf is a priestess of Zon-Kuthon and an agent of the Brotherhood of Bones — Kuthite fanatics sworn to secure the relics of Kazavon so no one can ever resurrect the warlord. Her 'hunt for the criminal Andaisin' was cover for a relic-search; she instead found Kazavon's influence saturating seven local artists — and, most potent of all, the city's new queen. When Ileosa appeared publicly wearing fangs on her crown, Laori was stunned; she now watches and waits while her superiors deliberate.

Sanctioned early reveal. The intended endgame revelation — that the fangs on Ileosa's crown are Kazavon's teeth, and that is why she cannot be killed — is normally learned from Neolandus at the chapter's end. The AP explicitly blesses letting the PCs learn it early from Laori instead (under magical compulsion, or if she comes to trust them). Nothing breaks: the PCs still need the seneschal to learn how such power can be fought.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, B1-B2 — p.146.

The smell in this entryway is of must and mildew, much of it coming from the mud tracked over the floorboards, as if a small army had marched through the room. To the south stands a single empty set of shelves.

A single bed, the blankets and pillows atop it scattered and in disarray, sits to the south in this room. More muddy boot prints mar the wooden floor here and a splash of dried blood decorates one pillow.

@Check[type:survival|dc:24] (front room): a half-dozen people tromped through — and a seventh was dragged out, toes scuffing. @Check[type:survival|dc:24] (bedroom): the prints converge on the bed — Salvator was clubbed in his sleep (the blood) and carried off. The trail is days old and leads toward central Old Dock: Pilts's Palace.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, B3 — p.146.

Both of this room's windows are tightly shuttered, yet the air inside seems strangely fresh and scented, no doubt because of the six large candles that burn within. Each candle has been affixed by a glob of melted wax to the crown of a gleaming, polished skull, and each of these impromptu and grisly candleholders has been placed atop an otherwise clear desk to the west, arrayed in a gentle arc. A chair sits before the desk, and a careful stack of papers and scrolls sits inside the arc of skulls. To the south stands a nearly empty cabinet, its shelves barren save for a few paintbrushes and a cracked pottery urn.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori Vaus} works here among the skulls of Kazavon's six suicides — left to right: Jeonia Chirco the costumer, Boathar Kaay the toymaker, Velaka Hoon the writer, Maxtel Erns the sculptor, Yvos Tanguany the tattooist, and Imon Vernell the poet (she names them fondly) — questioning the dead with mostly-garbled results. She saved Salvator for last, judging Kazavon's whispers strongest in his work, and arrived to find him already taken.

Her bargain: an alliance to reach Salvator, sealed by her evidence — a bloodstained sleeve-scrap bearing the city's coat of arms, found wedged behind the bed (@Check[type:society|dc:22]: it is from the uniform of the seneschal of Castle Korvosa — torn when Salvator splinted Neolandus's broken arm). She reveals the Emperor of Old Korvosa holds Salvator. Asked if she means the artist harm, she laughs the idea off, truthfully: she only wants to ask where his inspiration comes from.

What she admits vs. conceals: she owns her Zon-Kuthon faith freely if asked (his art shares her god's themes, she says) — but volunteers nothing of the Brotherhood of Bones, her suspicions of the queen, or Kazavon. Only magical compulsion pries those loose — unless you opt for the sanctioned early crown reveal (see the Artist's Lair overview). She is wariest of paladins and priests of Shelyn.

Story award: dealing with Laori peacefully and forming the alliance is worth XP as if the party had defeated her in combat — a full Severe encounter's award (PF1e 9,600 XP).

If rebuffed: she looks briefly hurt, bounces back, predicts they'll meet again, and leaves — then shadows the party, waiting to 'reappear' once they find Salvator, ideally by saving their lives in a fight gone bad to prove her good intentions.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, B4 — p.147.

This room appears to have once been a combination kitchen and storeroom, but is now a bewildering riot of brightly colored fungus and mold. The stuff grows everywhere and in every color, over tables, across cupboards, and in swaths along the floor and walls, but it seems thickest to the southwest, where puffy sheets of yellow fungus cover several objects so completely it's impossible to make out what lies beneath.

A patch of @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.hazards.Actor.A93flWUsot3FmC7t]{Yellow Mold} (the official L8 hazard) chokes the sunless northeast corner — Salvator handled his samples only in sunlight; his abductors didn't know to.

Beneath the growth, among the painting supplies: a full jar of marvelous pigments — the chapter's signature wondrous treasure.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, B5 — p.147.

What once might have been a small house has been obliterated, collapsed from within by a twenty-foot-diameter sinkhole. Water from the Narrows fills the hole, creating a muddy pit strewn with jagged bits of timber and flotsam. The edges of the hole are slick and patchy with fungus. Immediately south of the sinkhole, the boardwalk has also collapsed, creating a dangerous tangle of timber and crazily tilted pilings.

The quarantine ended the city's otyugh-control; several burst up a drainage tunnel here. The mud bottom is difficult terrain; the slick slope takes @Check[type:athletics|dc:14] to scale under pressure.

  • 3× @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary.Actor.kPBSSOuR8n8vZHXB]{Otyugh (4)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori Vaus (10, ally)}
Sewer otyughsTrivial · 30 XP @ L8

3 @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary.Actor.kPBSSOuR8n8vZHXB]{otyughs} wallow and lurch out at passersby (apply the Elite adjustment for a meaner fight). If the PCs haven't met @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori} yet, she bursts from B3 a round in, shrieking with delight, and joins the fight to earn goodwill.

pages.name: Part 2 — The Emperor of Old Korvosa

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, Part 2 — Emperor of Old Korvosa — p.150.

Part 2. Pilts Swastel — failed impresario of the gore-playhouse Exemplary Execrables — has crowned himself Emperor of Old Korvosa. He rules Old Dock by spectacle: real-death 'plays,' the mob game blood pig, and an ornately carved guillotine (the 'tall knife') with a standing appetite. When volunteers run short, he invents traitors.

Researching the Emperor: each attempt takes 1d4 hours and a Society check to gather and sift the island's strangled gossip (treat it as nearly impossible from off the island). Results are cumulative:

  • @Check[type:society|dc:19] — the Emperor rules Old Dock from a seized tenement 'palace' on Silk Street and rarely leaves it. His fanatics sweep Old Dock for conscripts; those who refuse are taken anyway — for other purposes.
  • @Check[type:society|dc:22] — Old Korvosa is his stage: prisoners are forced into deadly games and gruesome performances against his pets and followers, and the rest are beheaded by his favourite toy, an extravagant guillotine he calls the 'Tall Knife.'
  • @Check[type:society|dc:24] — an audience must be earned with entertainment. His current obsession is a brutal game of his own invention, blood pig, played on a converted rooftop field in the palace every evening; the howling of players and crowd carries across Old Dock.
  • @Check[type:society|dc:29] — the Emperor's real name: Pilts Swastel, pre-quarantine owner of the notorious Old Korvosa theater Exemplary Execrables.

His 'palace' is a block of converted tenements in central Old Dock. Salvator is there. So is the audience the PCs will need to play to — or fight through.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C — Pilts's Palace — p.151.

C. Pilts's Palace — a block of six gutted tenements at 11 Silk Street, central Old Dock, linked by rope bridges and rooftop fields, where the Emperor holds court from an hour past sunrise to dusk. The intended path is showmanship, not siege: petition the Emperor, accept the blood pig wager, win an audience with Salvator. Fighting the whole court at once is an Extreme-tier mistake — Extreme · 180 XP @ L8.

Getting in: guards who spot unescorted PCs raise the alarm and demand their purpose. @Check[type:diplomacy|dc:26] or @Check[type:intimidation|dc:22] talks the thugs into escorting the party to the Emperor (a critical success and they don't insist on confiscating weapons first). Mob-escorted PCs enter at C1 and are marched up to the game-field (C4) before the throne, every thug alert. If it comes to blades: a guard battle can spread palace-wide with Pilts barking orders from his throne — but defeating the Emperor cows the entire mob; nobody needs to kill every thug, and Pilts can summon hundreds of replacements from Old Dock besides.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C1-C2 — p.152.

The interior of this home has been gutted. A huge mound of rubble-broken timbers, bits of wall, ruined furniture, and other debris-lies heaped in the center of the room. Rickety wooden stairs wind up to a splintered hole in the wall near the roof above.

What was once an attic has been cleared of all clutter, leaving a large open area under exposed rafters and the roof above. Rope bridges lead to other areas outside of the room to the northeast and southwest, and a flight of stairs descends to a lower floor to the northwest.

The guardroom holds 4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thugs} (−2 to their Perception — they're arguing about whether chokers have skeletons). The NE bridge runs level; the SW one climbs an extra 10 ft to the throne balcony as an unsettling 'rope stairway'; the NW stairs descend into rubble that blocks all entry from below.

  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thug (5)}
GuardroomLow · 60 XP @ L8

4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thugs} — posted only while the alarm is down.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C3 — p.153.

This open-air balcony is shielded from rain and sun by a brightly colored canvas roof that extends up over the area like a half-dome, held in place by a wooden framework. The inside of the canvas has been decorated with scenes of gruesome debauchery; battlefields, executions, torture chambers, and human-eating monsters all vie for space. The balcony contains two major features of note. The first is a high-backed throne that looks like a poor man's version of the Crimson Throne, a thing of blood-red cushions and silks and spikes. Directly west of the throne stands an intimidating device: a tall guillotine of carved wood and bone, its base depicting grasping demonic feet and the housing of its glittering blade a leering demonic face.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.piltsSwastel0001]{Pilts Swastel} holds court here daily with @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.jabbyrGnome00001]{Jabbyr} (in executioner's costume at the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.tallKnifeGuillo1]{Tall Knife}) and 4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{thugs}, hearing mob reports, citizen pleas, and execution dockets.

Negotiating: Pilts happily confirms Salvator is his 'houseguest' but refuses every plea and threat — until boredom suggests the wager (see A Rousing Game). An unnoticed suggestion-style spell can move him; a noticed one means the Tall Knife.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.piltsSwastel0001]{Pilts Swastel (10)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.jabbyrGnome00001]{Jabbyr (8)}
  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Thug (5)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.tallKnifeGuillo1]{The Tall Knife (10)}
The full court (DON'T)Extreme · 180 XP @ L8

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.piltsSwastel0001]{Pilts (10)} + @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.jabbyrGnome00001]{Jabbyr (8)} + 4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{thugs} + a mob of spectators — an Extreme bloodbath best avoided until the blood pig audience isolates Pilts (see the Development under Blood Pig).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C4-C5 — p.154.

The large, flat roof of this long building has been converted into a strange sort of marshaling yard or game field. Two large square areas have been outlined with what could be blood, while at either end to the west and east, small wooden cages have been set up to the side of a hole in the roof that drops into the upper floor of the building below. Flights of stairs lead up from the roof to a balcony to the north and the gently slanted roof of a building to the south.

The blood pig field: cages (C4a/C4e) over trapdoors; wolverine pits (C4b/C4f — one starving @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary-2.Actor.6YOyTHRGqkYl2Prb]{wolverine} each); team squares (C4c — Shinglesnipes, C4d — visitors). The game's name rotates with its animal (blood dog, blood cat, and blood rat all had their runs); pig squeals please the Emperor most.

Two gently sloped rooftops overlook a flat roof to the north. The northern slopes of these roofs are littered with boards, stacks of shingles, and other impromptu seats, transforming the area into a sort of arena-style seating. The southern slopes are falling into disrepair, and it's obvious the materials to build the northern sides' seating were harvested from there.

C5, the spectator rooftops, hold 4 patrolling @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{thugs} (a pair per roof) when no game runs — and dozens of howling lowlifes during one.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C6 — p.155.

Crates, boxes, and barrels fill this long storeroom, making it difficult to judge the room's actual dimensions.

@Check[type:perception|dc:26] turns up a small jewelry box of worthless costume glass — and a real ring of chameleon power (quirk: activating or removing it turns all the wearer's hair a random unnatural colour; Pilts refused to wear it).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C7 — p.155.

This large room might once have been an attic storage area, but the rafters above now brood over an empty chamber. To the northwest, a large portion of the roof and floor below have collapsed entirely, leaving a void looking out over the sodden skyline of Old Korvosa.

Six @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.chokerBrute00001]{choker brutes} — Pilts's corpse-disposal partners since the theater days — nest here. The collapsed gap can be a stealth entry (@Check[type:athletics|dc:24] to climb the 25-ft exterior), but within 5 feet of the broken floor a square crumbles: @Check[type:reflex|dc:20] to lurch back or fall for @Damage[2d6[bludgeoning]] (and the noise).

  • 6× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.chokerBrute00001]{Choker Brute (5)}
The nestModerate · 90 XP @ L8

6 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.chokerBrute00001]{choker brutes} strike from the rafters — at the door from outside, or 3 rounds after southern intruders start moving. They fight to the death.

@Check[type:perception|dc:22] finds their bag wedged in the SE rafters: ~75 gp re-scaled in coin, a pearl-inset silk glove, a masterwork hand crossbow, and a wand of slow (13 charges).

pages.name: C8-C10. Stairway, Art Supplies & Trophy Hall

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C8-C10 — p.156.

C8 is a rubble-choked stairway — the only obstacle is the 1d4 hours it takes to clear a path to the abandoned floor below.

Several barrels and crates sit against the walls here. Stacked on some are many blank canvases and what appear to be ceramic containers. A stack of paintings leans against the eastern wall, stretched on wooden frames and covered with sheets.

The new Screams: @Check[type:society|dc:24] (or Art Lore) identifies the stacked paintings as brand-new work by Salvator Scream; beating the DC by 10 reveals something more — they're muddled and pedestrian beside his pre-quarantine brilliance, the trademark blue pigment applied sloppy and thin.

Why (GM only): when Kazavon's spirit-fragment bonded with Queen Ileosa, it abandoned the artists it had been cultivating. Salvator's muse is gone — the same clue written across his cell's failures. If @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori} is along she gleefully riffles the whole stack, is crestfallen at how ordinary they are, and wants nothing more to do with them.

C9 treasure: 11 new Scream paintings — same beloved themes (shadowy dragons, torture, vivid despair), little of the old power — worth ~2 gp each re-scaled.

C10 is the trophy hall:

The air in this room smells sickly sweet-a combination of flowers and vinegar. The unpleasant smell likely comes from the fourteen poorly preserved heads mounted on the walls of this grisly trophy hall. Most of the heads are human, although two are those of elves and one is from a dwarf with a beard roughly sheered off to match the cut to the neck. To the north, a small, child-sized bed sits against the wall opposite a wooden table decorated with a magnificent set of silverware.

Pilts talks to the heads on sleepless nights; the small bed is Jabbyr's. The C11 door carries an alarm spell keyed to Pilts.

The table is set with a full silverware service (~50 gp re-scaled) and a platinum-and-crystal brandy decanter (~75 gp).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C11 — p.157.

This extravagantly decorated bedroom would seem to belong in the richest of noble villas or monarchs' castles-at least, until one looks a little more closely at the sheets on the four-poster bed and notes how stained and frayed they are, or examines the tapestries and bed curtains and sees the patches of mold and threadbare edges. A tall, well-stocked bookcase to the south turns out to be leaning against the wall for support; the contents of its sagging shelves are poorly produced books with violent or erotic names on their mildewed spines.

Everything is slightly musty, stained with age, and well beyond its prime. Only the three paintings hanging on the wall hold up to closer examination, but their grisly subject matter might make them difficult to show in most public venues. To the north, a simple wooden door is secured with a lock and a heavy wooden bar.

Pilts sleeps and reads here after dark, and for an hour each night unbolts the C12 door to feed Salvator and collect finished paintings.

The three Scream originals (painted before his muse fell silent — and full of buried truth): a shadow-clad figure at a dolmen (@Check[type:religion|dc:26]: Zon-Kuthon entering ancient Nidal; ~45 gp); desert riders over skull-dunes (@Check[type:religion|dc:29]: a scene from the Umbral Leaves; ~110 gp); a man peeling away his own flesh to reveal blue scales (@Check[type:survival|dc:29] places the horizon-castle as Scarwall in Belkzen; @Check[type:arcana|dc:29] matches the scales to a blue dragon; ~200 gp). If @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori} is along she wants all three, settles for a split — and keeps offering to buy the PCs' share.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, C12 — p.158.

The air in this room is an unpleasant mix of body odor and paint. A lumpy straw mattress lies on the floor in one corner of the room, partially covered by a few blankets, while in the other stands a large easel on which rests a nearly completed painting of immense fiends attacking a village.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.salvatorScream01]{Salvator Scream} paints here under lock, bar, and beating. He begs rescue of any visitor who isn't the Emperor — but says nothing of Neolandus while Pilts lives. His price: the Emperor's death, and escort to the mainland (@Check[type:diplomacy|dc:19] with a credible rescue promise; Intimidation or magic also work — @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.laoriVaus0000001]{Laori} cheerfully recommends both). Even then he won't finish the story until he's somewhere he can't be overheard.

Wards & what becomes of him: every morning Pilts re-casts an alarm on this cell's door — a mental ping warns the Emperor the instant anyone but him opens it (on top of the warded C10 door). And mind the artist's nerve: below half HP he bolts for any small dark hole, and if he slips away on his own he eventually creeps home to 140 Wave Street — where the sinkhole otyughs (B5) eat him, unless the PCs already cleared them.

Laori's payoff. She has questions she'd rather ask Salvator privately: where do the ideas come from? Learning that his muse spoke in vivid dreams — and that the dreams have stopped — excites, then deflates her. She means him no harm (a living artist's muse might someday return), and she volunteers to escort Salvator to the mainland — a promise this priestess of the god of pain genuinely keeps. Errand done, she takes her leave to report to her superiors, enigmatically predicting the PCs haven't seen the last of her (true — Chapter 5), and slips into the lengthening shadows.

Story award: learning what Salvator knows — the Neolandus/Arkona revelation below — is worth a Moderate encounter's XP (PF1e 4,800).

His testimony is the chapter's hinge: Ileosa murdered the king; Neolandus knows; the Arkonas have him. On to Part 3 — and the rescue earns the trail to Arkona Palace.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, A Rousing Game / Blood Pig! — p.158.

The wager. Pilts offers it when pleading stalls: beat his champion team — the eight-thug Shinglesnipes — at blood pig, and the PCs get five supervised minutes with Salvator (plus, if they surrendered their gear at the door, its return and safe escort from Old Dock). The rules were invented by a madman, are never written down, and change weekly — narrate Pilts declaiming them; confusion is part of the show.

The rules (as declaimed this week): first team to 5 points; a point = your team's pig into your team's pit. No weapons, no spellcasting (each violation = a point to the other team); unarmed Strikes are legal. Up to 8 a side, starting in the marked squares; a fresh pig is craned into the cage opposite the scoring team's pit; a coin flip starts play.

  • Picking up a pig (cage or loose): an Interact action that provokes reactions; a loose pig also needs a successful unarmed Strike vs AC 15 to corner.
  • Carrying a live pig: each round roll d6 on the Squirming Pig table — 1 slump (lose 1 action) · 2 squirm (@Check[type:athletics|dc:20] or drop) · 3 squeal (the crowd roars) · 4 bite (pig attacks +6, 1 damage; if damaged, check to drop) · 5 kick (@Check[type:acrobatics|dc:20] or drop) · 6 panic (all four at once). Taking damage while carrying: @Check[type:athletics|dc:15] (+1 to the DC per point of damage taken) or drop. Thugs knock their pigs out first.
  • Loose pigs bolt 30 ft from the nearest creature each round (never off the roof or into a pit). Pass = Interact to an adjacent ally; throw = improvised −2 Strike, 10-ft increments; catch @Check[type:athletics|dc:18]; intercept (reaction) @Check[type:athletics|dc:24]; steal = a Disarm attempt, then the table.
  • Scoring: drop into an adjacent pit free, or throw at the 1-ft mouth (a Strike vs AC 15). The pits are 10 ft deep with a starving @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary-2.Actor.6YOyTHRGqkYl2Prb]{wolverine} in each — killing a wolverine forfeits for the killer's team; it spends 1d3 rounds devouring each scored piglet, otherwise claws at anything within 5 ft of the mouth.
  • Betting: the thugs give 2-to-1 against the PCs, up to 100 gp per PC.
  • 8× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Shinglesnipe (5)}
  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary-2.Actor.6YOyTHRGqkYl2Prb]{Wolverine (2)} (in the pits)
BLOOD PIG — the ShinglesnipesSevere · 120 XP @ L8

8 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.emperorsThug0001]{Emperor's Thugs} (the Shinglesnipes) — but it's a game, not a battle: unarmed, pig-fixated, crowd-roared. Run it fast and absurd.

Development — the sore loser. On a PC win Pilts demands two-of-three and adds @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.jabbyrGnome00001]{Jabbyr} to the Shinglesnipes (player cap be damned); after a second loss he calls for three-of-five — and is told they're out of pigs. He nearly guillotines the messenger, then laughs, congratulates the PCs, and brings them up the balcony with only Jabbyr and two thugs. The supervised meeting (Salvator silent while Pilts lives) is the PCs' best shot at Pilts away from his mob: Moderate · 110 XP @ L9.

Payouts & awards: the thugs honor their 2-to-1 bets — up to 200 gp profit per wagering PC per game. Winning two games and the audience with Salvator is itself worth a Severe encounter's XP (PF1e 9,600) on top of XP for any thugs bested during play.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, Part 3 — Wrath of the Arkonas — p.162.

Part 3. Salvator's testimony points across Old Korvosa to Arkona Palace, where both Neolandus and Vencarlo sit in the dungeons below — insurance policies in a rakshasa's game.

The Arkona secret: generations ago, on a Vudra trade voyage, the human Arkonas were slaughtered and replaced by rakshasas. 'Glorio' is the rakshasa Bahor; his 'sister Meliya' is Vimanda (Verik's 'lover' from Chapter 1) — rivals locked in covert sibling war beneath a flawless family front. The palace crawls with lesser rakshasas and magically-thralled human 'relatives' who believe the family merely criminal.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D — Arkona Palace — p.162.

D. Arkona Palace — the Vudrani-styled seat atop Endrin Isle, all topiary tigers, sandalwood censers, everburning torches, and 20-ft ceilings. Only 'family' lives inside. @Check[type:religion|dc:30] identifies the door-carvings as Chamidu, Vudrani goddess of wild beasts.

Securing an audience: a believable request gets a six-guard escort to the entry, where the one-eyed majordomo Carnochan (a @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.maraiRakshasa001]{marai}, telepathically bonded to Bahor) receives visitors; the PCs wait in the lounge (D2) under Bahor's scrying. Five minutes later 'Glorio' appears — concerned, charming, mind-reading. His deal: he wants Ileosa gone (Neolandus is his legal lever, for later) but can't look weak before Vimanda — so: kill the Emperor of Old Korvosa, and he'll help the PCs find their friends.

Bahor's Offer (after Pilts dies): a congratulation in the baths (D15), the gift of a ring of evasion (its deep-green bloodstone is his spy-eye), and the truth: both 'guests' are in the Vivified Labyrinth (@Check[type:society|dc:25] recalls its rumors). He reveals the D4 elephant-passage, the passphrase 'Chamidu is blind,' and the rotation levers — and secretly sends @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{Vimanda} down to die at the PCs' hands (a sending scroll burned as they leave, the trap framed to her as an honour). Story award: learning the prisoners' location by negotiation, without violence, is worth a full Severe encounter's XP (PF1e 19,200).

Where are the prisoners? Vencarlo in E20, Neolandus in E21 — under a permanent mage's private sanctum (no scrying), kept unconscious (sending fails; dream reports only 'alive, sleeping').

Infiltration logic: Bahor lets his house test intruders, intervening only at his chambers or treasury (then pitching the deal). On alarm: humans flee; Avishandu + Nudhaali hold the garden invisible; Carnochan + Vennashti sweep rooms in order; the marai fight human-shaped until one dies — then all revert and fight to the death. If the PCs retreat, nothing pursues: the rakshasas resume their human masks, give their dead to Avidexu in D24 — and a day later a scroll-borne sending reaches a PC:

'Greetings. This is Glorio Arkona. I apologize for the misunderstanding with my unusual servants. I wish to speak to you in private at my palace.'

The cover story: PCs who accept are received graciously; 'Glorio' explains away what they saw as bound rakshasas kept as bodyguards — a noble family's regrettable but necessary precaution — asks for their discretion, and tries to recruit them with the same kill-Pilts bargain. PCs who refuse the invitation leave him to his defenses: he withdraws to D18 and lets the palace repel intruders, attacking personally only if they grind through it room by room. (More rakshasas could be summoned from Vudra — but not within this chapter.)

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D1-D3 — p.162.

A black marble arch depicting dozens of elephants standing one atop the other frames a great ebony door in the south wall of this wide and airy entrance hall. Above the door, an elephant looks out over the hall, its single eye a glittering bloodstone the size of an apple. Tall windows grant a commanding view of the palace grounds, and a rich red carpet, ten feet wide and luxuriously thick, provides a pathway between doors to the west and north, and around a corner to the east. Each of these doors, as well as three smaller ones to the west of the northern pair, are decorated with images of a six-armed, four-faced woman who rides a tiger with human hands for feet.

A permanent magic mouth trumpets (and the elephant-head animates) for any visitor not wearing the Arkona crest. At night the front doors are secured with a good lock (@Check[type:thievery|dc:30]). The fireplace-warmed lounge (D2) is the waiting room — and the watching room.

A fourteen-foot-tall marble statue of a six-armed woman with four faces on her head-one looking to each of the four cardinal directions-stands under a dome of colored glass. The statue wields numerous weapons in its six hands, and a small kneeler at its base gives a place to pay respects to whatever deity the statue represents. Each of the statue's eight eyes is a sparkling bright green orb.

@Check[type:religion|dc:30] names the exotic statue: Chamidu, Vudrani goddess of wild beasts.

The third-eye network: nine third eye bloodstones stud the palace (the elephant's eye ~100 gp; the statue's west-face eye, a fountain cobra, a hookah, the wax-corpse 'heart,' a javelin head, the treasury watch-stone, Bahor's paperweight, and the gifted ring) — every one a lens for @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.bahorArkona00001]{Bahor}; their faint divination auras are masked (magic aura).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D4 — p.163.

This chamber hardly seems to be part of a palace, but looks more like a clearing at the heart of a vast jungle, teeming with life. The sky above is a deep, cloudless blue, while in the distance, hazy towers rise above the verdant canopy. Exotic bird calls fill the air, the scent of dozens of unfamiliar flowers and plants assault the nose, and everywhere a riot of color demands the eye, whether it's the wing of a tropical bird, the petals of a brightly hued flower, or the glittering multicolored tiles that make up a round fountain to the north.

The fountain's central plume is a stone pillar around which entwine two cobra statues that clutch green gems in their fanged maws. To the south of the fountain stands an immense, life-sized jade statue of an elephant, a howdah perched on its back, its tusks and trunk raised high in greeting to the southeast doors. From inside the room, the doors look more like gates set into a wrought-iron fence that encircles the garden.

Other gates set in this fence doubtless lead to other parts of the palace, and after a bit more observation, the somewhat static nature of the jungle and landscape becomes apparent-the walls of this garden are in fact an incredibly realistic and clever painting of a Vudrani junglescape.

Illusion-dressed windows keep the jungle seamless; @Check[type:perception|dc:23] picks the real balcony from the painted walls. The door to the palace grounds is kept secured with a good lock (@Check[type:thievery|dc:30]). The cobra-fountain holds two bloodstone 'eyes' (one a spy-stone); its plume is an officially-statted @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.1AZoRkLec47xrTqY]{living waterfall} that ignores intruders — unless they open the secret trap door.

The way down: @Check[type:perception|dc:28] finds the circular platform under the elephant statue; a hidden pressure-plate rotates it open onto spiral stairs (10 minutes, then auto-close; a second switch 20 steps down reopens it). Without the passphrase 'Chamidu is blind,' the statue — a @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.I00S3LWnDJfCn4zv]{marble elephant guardian} (a 26,000-gp variant figurine of wondrous power that can also hold a statue-spell form indefinitely) — animates and attacks.

  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.I00S3LWnDJfCn4zv]{Marble Elephant (7)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.1AZoRkLec47xrTqY]{Living Waterfall (5)}
  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.maraiRakshasa001]{Marai Rakshasa (9)}
Garden on alertLow · 75 XP @ L10

The @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.I00S3LWnDJfCn4zv]{marble elephant} + the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.1AZoRkLec47xrTqY]{living waterfall} + Avishandu and Nudhaali (2 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.maraiRakshasa001]{marai}, invisible in the howdah, re-casting every 5 minutes before abandoning post). Avishandu directs the elephant; Nudhaali rains scorching rays.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D5-D8 — p.165.

D5 holds garden tools, five huge water casks, and buckets by the dozen; the D6 storerooms (three of them) stack tools, firewood, and food among barrels and crates — fine hiding spots for sneaking PCs.

Two floor-to-ceiling bookshelves decorate the south and west walls of this chamber, facing a curved north wall on which hang a half-dozen grotesque paintings depicting torture, murder, cannibalism, and even necrophilia. Two glittering chandeliers hang from the ceiling, providing more than enough lighting to read by for anyone sprawled on the dozens of cushions strewn about the room.

The library shelves poetry beside conjuration treatises in no order at all; many volumes are in Vudrani. The six wall paintings are Salvator Scream originals — among his most stomach-turning, the trademark blue everywhere — worth ~80 gp each re-scaled.

A long couch and two stuffed chairs sit around an elegant rug on the floor of this room, the walls of which are carved in depictions of air spirits cavorting in the clouds. The scent of exotic smoke lingers in the air, and four bejeweled hookahs sit in the room, two at either end of the couch, and one next to each chair. A flight of stairs leads up to the floor above, and a glass case built into the underside of these stairs displays a wide collection of cigars, pottery jars, and bundles of dried leaves in a range of colors.

D8 treasure: the four bejeweled hookahs run ~35 gp each (the one by the southwestern chair hides a 100-gp-grade third eye spy-stone in its pipe); the case holds 10 pounds of exotic cigars, smoking-weed, and minor narcotics worth ~140 gp re-scaled — and is warded by the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.hungrySmokeTrap1]{Hungry Smoke} trap (Bahor keeps the only key).

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.hungrySmokeTrap1]{Hungry Smoke (8)}
  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.belkerSmoke00001]{Belker (7)}
Hungry Smoke springsTrivial · 30 XP @ L10

2 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.belkerSmoke00001]{belkers} from the 20-ft ceiling — reach down, can't easily be reached back.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D9-D10 — p.166.

Glass cases line this hallway's north and south walls. The northern case displays exotic weapons, many of which resemble swords, spears, and starknives, hanging on the wall around a suit of bright blue leather and ivory armor. The southern case displays a gruesome tableau of a stuffed tiger feeding upon a extremely realistic sculpture of a dead Vudrani man.

D9 treasure (cases locked, untrapped — @Check[type:thievery|dc:26], expert): the wax corpse's exposed 'heart' (@Check[type:society|dc:20] to appraise from afar) is a carved third eye bloodstone, ~50 gp re-scaled. North case: a +1 ghost touch kama, 12 masterwork daggers, a masterwork falchion, six punching daggers, two sais, two sianghams, two spears — and the armor, +1 resilient studded leather in blue leather and ivory.

A gracefully curving wall lined with tall windows presents a commanding view of the western palace grounds. The room is empty save for three human-sized statues, each depicting a Vudrani warrior holding aloft a glittering blue-green javelin with a head carved from bloodstone.

The house alarm: each statue bears a magic mouth that erupts in insulting Vudrani oaths the moment any creature carries a drawn weapon into the room — instantly alerting the household (especially any marai resting in D14). Prying a javelin loose is easy, but triggers that statue's mouth all the same.

D10 treasure: the three bloodstone javelin heads are worth ~15 gp each re-scaled — and the central statue's is a third eye spy-stone, another lens for @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.bahorArkona00001]{Bahor}.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D11-D13 — p.166.

A magnificent mahogany table dominates this room, the surface of which is decorated by an immense cover fashioned from the brightly colored skins of various great snakes.

No servant ever enters during dinner ('a restrictive Vudrani custom' — and convenient, since now and then the family prefers to feed in true form behind locked doors, tidying up with prestidigitation). The grounds doors are locked (@Check[type:thievery|dc:30]) and magic mouth-alarmed like D1, though without bloodstones.

D12, the upper balcony, overlooks the garden 25 feet below — warm air, birdsong, and fountain-babble drift up (and the D4 ambushers can target creatures here).

A large, ornate mirror hangs on the western wall of this washroom and latrine. The air smells remarkably fresh, and the water-filled porcelain commode to the east looks more comfortable than it has any right to be.

D13 comedy treasure: the magical toilet — minor magics freshen the air and transmute waste to clean water piped down to the garden — is technically worth ~15 gp re-scaled… and weighs some 300 pounds. Hauling it out of a rakshasa palace is left as an exercise for the party.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D14 — p.166.

The floor of this room is thickly carpeted and strewn with numerous throw pillows and furs. The walls are carved with erotic depictions of men and women engaged in all manner of sexual acts. The only real furnishings in the room are the four beds against the western walls.

Off-duty, the four 'Arkona cousins' lounge in true form (often with abducted 'entertainment'; the quarantine has made them short-tempered). Bickering, they take −2 Perception to hear the house alarms. On alert they defer to Vennashti's tactics.

  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.maraiRakshasa001]{Marai Rakshasa (9)}
The four maraiSevere · 120 XP @ L10

Avishandu, Carnochan, Nudhaali, Vennashti — 4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.maraiRakshasa001]{marai rakshasas}. A Severe brawl; worse if the alarm has scattered them into ambush pairs.

pages.name: D16. Leaves of Gold and Glitter (the Treasury)

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D16 — p.167.

The plain ebony walls of this chamber seem to glisten with moisture. Above, the chamber rises forty feet to a domed ceiling set with eight wedge-shaped windows; to the north, east, and south three additional glass windows rise up from floor level to the dome. The room is empty, save for a thirty-foot-tall tree with sparkling gold and green leaves, its roots burrowing in and out of the floor. A breeze tickles the branches above, and as the leaves waver, the sound of crystal bells filters through the room.

The vault: an iron door dressed as ebony (Hardness 10, HP 60; @Check[type:thievery|dc:40], master — a heightened arcane-locked lock; a silent mental alarm to Bahor; the walls, floor, and ceiling are sheathed in walls of force; a forbiddance bars planar tricks). @Check[type:religion|dc:30] names the illusion the Tree of Eternal Dawn, whose leaves grant eternal life at a terrible cost. A persistent screen hides the room's real contents (@Check[type:will|dc:23] on interaction): one iron chest (@Check[type:thievery|dc:40]), a bloodstone watch-eye atop it.

The chest: the Arkonas' true wealth is paper — oaths of loyalty, debt, and servitude, including the entire Cerulean Society roster in Arkona pockets and Glorio's letter ordering Guildmaster Boule to lie low so the desperate city ripens ('…and Old Korvosa shall be the Society's'). Enough to politically destroy the family — after Ileosa. Plus: ~650 gp re-scaled in coin and gems, a +1 holy light crossbow (cherrywood and ivory — kept, beautifully, by the creature it could kill), the ring of evasion (if never gifted) — and Vencarlo's confiscated gear: his clothes, purse, +1 rapier, signet ring, and the brass key to the Blackjack lockbox (area A8).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D17-D18 — p.169.

This austere chamber is apparently the sleeping chamber of someone quite orderly. The bed is made, sheets drawn tight against the mattress and pillows arranged perfectly at the head, and a dressing table and chair at the bed's side are both dust-free and polished to a shine.

Vimanda's room — austere to the point of insult. The 'loving' siblings privately despise each other; she has built her own underworld network to seize the family, plans curdled since the king's death, and lately lives in the dungeons venting on prisoners (@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{see her entry}).

The crowning glory of this dazzling chamber is, without a doubt, the four-posted canopy bed that takes up a full quarter of the room. Silk sheets, gossamer curtains, ebony bedposts depicting tigers and cobras chasing monkeys in an endless circle, and a half-dozen huge pillows combine to create a resting place fit for a king. Strange and ferocious animal heads are mounted on the walls, their eyes looking almost alive. A towering redwood cabinet stands against the eastern wall, while to the west sits a massive desk covered with books and scrolls and a large green paperweight the size of an apple. An exotic-looking, five-foot-long stringed instrument sits displayed on a bronze stand near the desk, and a spherical birdcage containing several brightly colored songbirds hangs from the center of the ceiling above.

D18, Bahor's room: the six 'Vudrani animal' heads (@Check[type:nature|dc:28]: not real animals; @Check[type:arcana|dc:30]: preserved rakshasa heads — his predecessors and disappointing children). During an infiltration @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.bahorArkona00001]{Bahor} waits here in human form, offering his deal one last time before the claws come out.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.bahorArkona00001]{Bahor (13)}
BAHOR (if it comes to it)Severe · 120 XP @ L10

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.bahorArkona00001]{Bahor} alone is Severe at L10 — with marai support, Extreme. The chapter neither expects nor requires his death; per the conclusion, he likely survives in damage-control (secret safe) or vendetta (secret out) mode.

Exquisite furniture (~300 gp re-scaled, unwieldy), a 500-gp bloodstone paperweight (spy-stone), and a gold-filigreed Jawassan sitar (~35 gp).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, D19-D25 — p.170.

The air in this vast grotto is cool. An iron-framed flight of spiral stairs winds up through a hole in the ceiling in the northwest section of the room from the top of a semicircular ledge. The ledge winds down along the cavern's inner wall to a series of rope bridges that descend even lower from ledge to ledge. The upper ledge is breathtaking and strangely beautiful-a tableau of all manner of odd-colored fungi, lichens, and molds. The fungi have been cultivated, shaped into symmetrical patterns normally not seen in nature, transforming the ledge into a sort of underground fungal garden. Here and there, flickering torches burn in sconces above the fungi, while from somewhere in the darkness below comes the soft splash of water against an unseen shore.

The natural sea-cave beneath the palace — the reason the Arkonas built here. The ledge spirals from the D4 stairs (100 ft above the water) down rope bridges (D20, the 'Cat's Cradle') past Avidexu's room (D21 — his animal carvings: a blue-coral gecko, a redwood cougar, a pearl-eyed gold flame drake, ~170 gp the set) to the pier.

The Innachi Naven hook: the four guardians below were once living janni slaves, murdered into fungal puppetry by the wizard Innachi Naven — a hireling who learned what the Arkonas really are and was handed to Senshiir (E21) as 'entertainment' for his trouble. Bahor tried and failed to craft more guardians himself; PCs who piece this together hold one more thread of the family's buried bodies.

  • 4× @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.gardenGuardian01]{Garden Guardian (8)}
D19 — the Garden GuardiansModerate · 80 XP @ L10

4 @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.gardenGuardian01]{Garden Guardians} — invisible, opening with overlapping spore-clouds on the narrow ledge. They never pursue into D24/D25 or the palace.

At the bottom of the vast cavern, a wooden pier extends out into a gently sloshing pool of blue seawater. A shallowdraft barge is docked at the pier, an unlit lantern hanging from its bow. Supporting timbers and brickwork line parts of the lower edge of the immense cavern to both the west and south, showing where the original sea cave has been artificially expanded. To the north, a twenty-five-foot-wide waterway provides an aquatic exit from the cavern-the distant, muted sounds of the surf and sloshing water echo down from that direction.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.enormousReefclaw]{Enormous Reefclaw (12)}
D23 — the Arkona Pier (the escape scene)Moderate · 80 XP @ L10

The 15-ft-deep pool is home to the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.enormousReefclaw]{Enormous Reefclaw} (50% out hunting on the first visit; always home for the escape — run this as the chapter's last battle, Vencarlo and Neolandus huddled aboard the barge). The waterway winds ~120 ft northwest to a 'stone wall' that is an illusion (@Check[type:will|dc:16] on interaction) masking the exit onto the Jeggare; the Arkonas use the cave to move sensitive cargo — most often unconscious abductees bound for the family table.

Four lit braziers illuminate this vast chamber, a cathedrallike space made all the more immense by its lack of benches or other concessions to comfort. The floor is polished tan marble, with the walls rising up to form a domed ceiling sixty feet above. Alcoves line these walls, six in all, inside of which stand human-sized statues of a tiger-headed man. To the south, a few steps lead up to a pulpit-like area with three more statues of the same figure-the central one being twice as tall and holding out before him two lances from which hang flags. The left is the flag of Korvosa, while the right bears the Arkona coat of arms.

  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary.Actor.hOXpmWmXU8N3n4Bw]{Avidexu (10)}
  • 2× @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core-2.Actor.pzCgOvNPish9QlTC]{Emperor Cobra (5)}
D24 — the Arkona 'Temple'Trivial · 40 XP @ L10

No god is worshiped here — every statue is Bahor as god-king of Korvosa, a monument to rakshasa ego. @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-bestiary.Actor.hOXpmWmXU8N3n4Bw]{Avidexu} (a true rakshasa, indifferent to politics, devoted to his pets) shares a fresh-shed snakeskin meal with two 18-ft @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core-2.Actor.pzCgOvNPish9QlTC]{emperor cobras}, which he feeds on animals and vagrants. D22 hides the dungeon door (@Check[type:perception|dc:25]; a tunnel winds 100 ft southeast to E1); D25 is the second tunnel to E1.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E — The Vivified Labyrinth — p.176.

E. The Vivified Labyrinth — the Arkonas' rotating dungeon-engine, built for their own entertainment: testing recruits, punishing prisoners. (Bahor's favourite game: a prisoner, a knife, a loincloth, a slow venom — and the promise of a curing pool somewhere deep inside. No one has ever won.) Four great central sections turn 90° when any rotation lever is pulled (E3, E8, E14, E16, E17 — the E14 lever is hidden), reshuffling every route. Below, in the Gizzard (E23), four chained skeletal mastodons walk the gears. Rooms are unlit, ceilings 10 ft unless noted. The party should be L10 entering.

The rotation has teeth. A pulled lever locks for 1 minute (@Check[type:thievery|dc:30], taking a full round at the mechanism, overrides the lockout for one extra pull). All four sections turn together over 1 round per 90°, bells ringing in the Gizzard and the grinding plainly audible through E1-E20 — and the openings between sections slam shut fast:

  • Caught astride a seam (a transitional square): @Check[type:reflex|dc:23] — on a success the creature flings itself 5 ft to either side, clear of the seam; on a failure the closing walls scissor it for @Damage[6d10[bludgeoning]] and shunt it into a random adjacent non-transitional square.
  • Riding a turning section: @Check[type:acrobatics|dc:20] to keep footing or fall @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.j91X7x0XSomq8d60]{Prone}.
  • @Check[type:perception|dc:23] at any seam reveals the tell-tale floor-and-ceiling gaps before the first pull.

The dungeon has four orientations; only the mapped first allows entry from E2 without teleportation. Sivit knows all four layouts and pulls levers to split parties.

Running the revolving dungeon: roll initiative when the PCs trigger the first rotation at E3 — and roll for @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.sivitDarksphinx1]{Sivit} and @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{Vimanda} too, tracking their movements secretly on their turns. Sketch the four mobile sections on four separate paper circles so each can physically rotate 90° at the table. If the PCs strand themselves away from every lever, have an NPC elsewhere pull one to keep the game afoot. Sanctioned static fallback: if rotation would only frustrate your group, throw it out entirely — connect E3-E4, E14-E15, and E17-E18 with tunnels and run the place as a fixed dungeon.

The two roamers: @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{Vimanda} — disguised as a starved, grateful Vencarlo ('Bahor sent you to die; come, we must face him!') — patrols clockwise from E13, tracking Bahor's gifted ring by locate object, immune to the symbol glyphs, terrified of Sivit. @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.sivitDarksphinx1]{Sivit} scries the lever rooms, herds prey across her glyphs, and cannot leave. Tell Sivit of Vimanda's treachery and the binding does the rest. If the PCs still carry the Chapter-1 raktavarna 'silver dagger,' it has been Vimanda's informant all along — and bites its owner when she calls it home.

pages.name: E1-E4. Entrance, Statues, First Lever & the Poisoned Pool

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E1-E4 — p.176.

E1: silent bronze tiger-doors; an illusory wall (@Check[type:will|dc:25] on interaction) hides the eastern passage. E2: two statues of a tiger-headed man — Bahor again (@Check[type:perception|dc:23] spots the rotation seam beyond). E3: the first ebony rotation lever. E4: a crystal pool whose water is a poison effect (@Check[type:fortitude|dc:25]; @Damage[6d6[poison]]; the magic is aura-masked, and water removed from it turns ordinary — Vimanda loves a well-timed suggestion here).

pages.name: E5-E8. Pools, Pain & the Second Lever

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E5-E8 — p.177.

E5: a murky pool that is actually a once-per-day heal (both roamers retreat here when hurt; liquid carried off reverts to foul water) — guarded by the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.symbolFear000001]{Symbol of Fear}. E6: a bare room whose floor bears the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.symbolPain000001]{Symbol of Pain}.

Three large wooden chests, their lids decorated with carvings of cavorting tigers, sit against one wall of this room. Some sort of message seems to be carved on each lid. Colorful frescoes on the wall opposite the chests depict hundreds of tigers marching in widening circles around a single green gem the size of a fist, set in the wall and carved to resemble a tiger's head.

The language gate: the lid inscriptions are carved in Vudrani — only readers of that tongue (or comprehend languages etc.) get Bahor's 'hints,' and since the hints mislead, speaking his native language is more curse than gift here. Left: 'By gentle caress shall truth be known.' Right: 'Breathe deep your salvation.' Middle: 'Life within but Death without.'

Left is the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.terinavChest0001]{terinav-painted decoy} (empty); right is the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.insanityChest001]{insanity-mist bellows}; the middle holds 3 healing potions + 3 lesser restoration potions and an underfed, furious @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.pathfinder-monster-core.Actor.AJ5LuNMVPLCydryP]{blood cobra} that strikes the first face it sees. The fresco's gem is a 75-gp-grade third eye bloodstone (re-scaled). E8: the second lever (Sivit scries this room after the first rotation).

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E9-E12 — p.178.

Four rooms built by Bahor's father (Bahor killed him; the rooms were too ingeniously cruel to demolish). Each has a hidden bypass switch (@Check[type:perception|dc:30]; suppresses 3 rounds, re-pressable):

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.bitingTigers0001]{The Biting Tigers (E9)} — snapping mounted heads on serpentine necks, four Strikes a round, watched by an invisible arcane eye; a secret door (@Check[type:perception|dc:30]) hides E10.

A ten-foot-wide, two-foot-tall well rises from the center of this circular room. Inky water fills the well nearly to its rim, obscuring its depths. A stone statue of a rearing snake rises from the center of the well. Along the length of the serpent's body, dozens of carved arms cross over the creature's belly-each arm grips a long curved blade. The statue's serpentine head rises ten feet above the surface of the water, gazing down coolly to the northeast with amethyst eyes.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.fangsDiomazul001]{The Fangs of Diomazul (E10)} — the 'well' is four inches of dark water over a blade-carousel; @Check[type:religion|dc:30] names the statue Diomazul, Serpent of Eighty Blades, an obscure Vudrani god of battle-fury and cruelty. The amethyst eyes pry out at ~50 gp each (a full round apiece).

Eight alcoves line this long, narrow hallway. Inside each alcove stands a human-sized, upright iron casket, the image of a sobbing woman decorating its lid. The hallway's floor gleams bright red, a mosaic of tiny red stones giving the appearance that the hall is awash in blood.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.wailingMaidens01]{The Wailing Maidens (E11)} — tilting floors dump victims into the kill-lane, the western maidens wail, the eastern fire spikes. @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.stingingWasps001]{The Stinging Wasps (E12)} — the wasp-swarm mosaic room, where thousands of six-inch needles stab in rippling waves.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E13-E17 — p.180.

E13, Meditation Chamber: a humming black-marble sonorous stone (@Check[type:occultism|dc:28] while reading auras): a minute within 5 feet grants calm emotions; touching it casts restoration AND restores up to 3 expended spell slots — four uses a month (it dies if removed). Vimanda's patrol starts here. E14: a bone-strewn room hiding a lever behind a secret door (@Check[type:perception|dc:30]). E15: murals that paint the viewer among the prey — pure dread. E17: Sivit's body-dump (the Arkonas carry the remains to table).

Four alcoves in the walls of this room contain floating spheres of mist, each hovering three feet off the ground. Each sphere is one foot in diameter and of a different color-black, white, green, and gold. Just north of the strange floating spheres, two levers protrude from opposite walls.

E16, Transport Room: the mist-spheres teleport a toucher (thrown objects too) — white→E2, black→E13, green→E20, gold→E5 — if the western lever is up. It starts down: every sphere instead dumps the toucher into a random unoccupied locked cell in E21 (no cells free → the spheres simply go dead). Superb mobility for those who know the pattern; a one-way oubliette for those who don't. The eastern lever is another rotation pull.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E18-E19 — p.181.

The two rooms that rotate open onto Sivit's chamber: the Hall of Slumber with its @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.symbolSleep00001]{Symbol of Sleep}, and the crooked Hall of Stunning with the @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-hazards.Actor.symbolStunning01]{Symbol of Stunning}. She times her entrances for the glyphs' harvest.

pages.name: E20. Sivit's Throne — the Vencarlo Rescue

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E20 — p.182.

A great green throne sits atop a dais in the northern end of the room. To either side stand statues of a tiger-headed man-each holds aloft a pair of chains from which manacles dangle. Dried blood spatters the walls, the floor, and even the throne and statues, filling the room with its stale reek.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.sivitDarksphinx1]{Sivit} keeps her playthings shackled to the statues (the dead go to E17). @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vencarloOrisini1]{Vencarlo Orisini} hangs from the western one — at 0 HP, starved, in rags, waking the moment anyone heals him. Battered as he is, his face lights up: 'It's quite the delight to see you.' He points out the manacle key under the throne cushion (@Check[type:perception|dc:26]; @Check[type:thievery|dc:30] without it). Healed, he insists on helping — Neolandus first — and, having spent time in the torture chamber, can show the PCs the illusory walls hiding the lower level.

The Blackjack confrontation: challenged about his other identity, Vencarlo laughs it off as a fine joke. Presented with proof (he was caught dressed as Blackjack), he goes quiet, then concedes — 'Looks like you caught me. Well done.' — and immediately defers the whole conversation: no time now; rescue Neolandus, get out of Korvosa, and there will be plenty of time to talk it over later. He keeps that promise.

Story award: rescuing Vencarlo is worth a Severe encounter's XP (PF1e 9,600).

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.sivitDarksphinx1]{Sivit (11)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vencarloOrisini1]{Vencarlo (0 HP)}
BOSS — Sivit, Lady of the LabyrinthLow · 60 XP @ L10

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.sivitDarksphinx1]{Sivit} — scry, rotate, herd, pounce. She wand-doors back here when bloodied and fights to the death inside her prison. Story award for Vencarlo's rescue.

pages.name: E21-E22. The Torture Chamber — the Neolandus Rescue

text.content:
Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E21 — p.184.

This large room is lit by a heartily burning firepit in the room's center. Cages hang on chains dangling from hooks in the ceiling, while racks, strappados, gibbets, and other implements of torture fill the floor space. A set of immense wooden doors stand in the wall to the north, while to the south stand several narrow cells.

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.senshiirAsura001]{Senshiir}, the four-armed asura who tortures for the Arkonas by contract, has just strapped a sobbing man to the rack — @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.neolandusKalep01]{Neolandus Kalepopolis} (@Check[type:society|dc:15] to recognize him; he claims to be 'Velak' otherwise, and begs the PCs to rescue 'a friend' before they leave). The E16 teleporters dump victims into the southern cells. E22 is the dead torturer's old room, where Senshiir sometimes meditates.

  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.senshiirAsura001]{Senshiir (10)}
  • @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{Vimanda (12, perhaps)}
Senshiir (+ Vimanda, if unalerted)Severe · 120 XP @ L10

@UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.senshiirAsura001]{Senshiir} alone is Low-Moderate — but if the PCs reached here without alarms or Bahor's deal, @UUID[Compendium.cotct-pf2e-conversion.cotct-actors.Actor.vimandaArkona001]{Vimanda} is here trading torture-craft with her, and the room is Severe-to-Extreme.

Story award: rescuing Neolandus from the rack is worth a Moderate-to-Severe encounter's XP (PF1e 6,400).

Once Neolandus is sure the PCs aren't the queen's agents, he grows excited — these are exactly the heroes he's been hoping for — and shares everything; see the Conclusion.

Source: CotCT (2016 HC), Ch.3, E23 — p.186.

This vast cavern stretches into the shadows, the true extent of its area difficult to discern due to a thick maze of wooden timbers that rise up to support the roof. A ledge winds along the eastern and northern sides of the cave, with the floor dropping away to a depth of thirty feet. Four immense stone pillars support the ceiling ten feet above. Where these pillars connect to the ceiling, a network of wooden braces and timbers radiate out in a wheel shape, forming four forty-footwide disks flush against the roof.
